data.title: Rheinmetall: Europe's Ammunition Powerhouse And Strong Buy Defense Investment
data.description:
Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBY) (OTCPK:RNMBF) is a German giant in the defense sector, and its expertise in land warfare products makes it the backbone for NATO supply in Europe. The company specializes in state-of-the-art ground vehicles, hardly replaceable by substitutes due to their reliability and proven combat effectiveness. This gives Rheinmetall a great strategic advantage over the competitors. At the same time, recent developments following the ReArm Europe plan and the need for replenishing NATO’s ammunition depots may make the company one of the protagonists in future European arms investments, thus making it a good long-term investment.

Europe’s need for ammunition

One of my main arguments in favor of Rheinmetall lies in the desperate need for ammunition in Europe. The war in Ukraine has drained the already scarce stockpiles of many NATO countries that were not ready for high-intensity warfare scenarios. In fact, the estimated number of shells fired in Ukraine lies around 5,000 per day, but the analysts agree that the real need is around 12,000. These numbers would be really hard to sustain with the current European rate of production, estimated at around 2 million per year.

Therefore, we can conclude that the demand is very high and will continue to be that high also in the case of a ceasefire in Ukraine. In this sense, the European Union recently presented the plan ReArm Europe, or Readiness 2030, which aims to invest €800 billion in armament production as deterrence for future Russian aggressions. The plan indicated ground vehicles, drones, and ammunition as the main objectives of production, with the funds destined only to EU-based companies.

Given the current scenario, we can say that Rheinmetall is in pole position to actively rebuild the backbone of EU ground forces and replenish drained stockpiles, using a significant amount of ReArm Europe’s €800 billion.

The company has already taken action. Actually, the company produces around 750,000 shells per year, with a goal of 1.1 million by 2027, or, in other words, more than half of European production. Secondly, recent news confirmed the already rumored launches of various plants in Northern Germany, Bulgaria, and Romania.

Rheinmetall launches Europe’s largest artillery ammo plant.

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

Romania, Rheinmetall to build $622M munitions powder plant.

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

Rheinmetall chief says Europe must catch up with Russia’s ammunition output as it opens mega plant.

(Source: CNBC)

Finally, it is important to say that Rheinmetall is also starting to convert existing plants to satisfy the demand. An example is the cooperation with Volkswagen:

Rheinmetall CEO visits VW plant as defence companies look to expand.

(Source: Reuters)

The Partnership with Anduril Industries

Another key issue of Western armed forces that many analysts pointed out after the war in Ukraine is the use of complex, costly, and time-consuming-to-produce weapons; this is the case with air-defense missiles and cruise missiles. This is a problem that came out also during the 12-day war between Iran and Israel when, in just a few days, hundreds of $2 million interceptors were used.

Rheinmetall probably saw this as an implicit demand in the market: mass-producible weapons with reduced costs. In my opinion, Rheinmetall stands out thanks to the recent partnership with Anduril Industries, a company that has made this idea its business model.

One of Anduril’s main products, the Barracuda cruise missile, could really stand out in the European market since it offers high performance with relatively low costs. To make a comparison, a standard cruise missile could cost around $1 million per unit, while Barracuda costs around $200,000 by using readily available components in the civilian aeronautical market. I don’t want to bore the reader with technicalities, but the point I want to make on Rheinmetall is clear: Europe in the future may need many cheap and easy-to-produce weapons, and the foresight of Rheinmetall has set the company in a dominant position compared to other European arms manufacturers.

Valuation

Regarding financials, the recent Q2FY25 results disappointed on sales, EBIT, and cash flow. This led the stock to drop more than 4%. In particular, the investors find the FCF disappointing at negative €991 million due to higher capex and inventory built-up. In my opinion, this should not worry the investors at all. This recent miss, combined with the recent stagnation of defense stock prices, could represent a good entry point to buy Rheinmetall at lower prices. In my opinion, the recent peace talks between Trump and Putin should not discourage investors from buying Rheinmetall since the actual defense spending trend will also continue in the future.

As for the valuation, the recent price decline put the company into undervalued territory. While the multiples may indicate overvaluation, with an EV/EBITA of 39.45x and a PE of 88.08, the PEG ratio of 1.35 may indicate the stock is fairly valued.

To avoid doing an incorrect valuation, I used a DCF model. In the tables below, I report the final calculations of WACC and the fair value of the stock.

My estimates are not far from the analysts’ consensus, as my Rheinmetall's fair value appears to be €1,993.58, slightly lower than the average consensus of €2,100.00. This difference may be explained by the two revenue growth rates I used, considering the fund spending of ReArm Europe until 2030. Finally, by doing simple math, the fair value of Rheinmetall quoted in USD is $2,399.60.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Rheinmetall seems to be one of the most promising stocks in Europe, due to several growth drivers. First of all, the ReArm Europe plan guarantees strong demand in the years to come. Secondly, the strategic initiatives the board has taken highlight the possibility for the company of becoming truly a European giant with a strong competitive advantage against close competitors. In this sense, I am referring to the possibility of becoming Europe’s most important ammunition company as well as a driver of innovation in highly scalable future arms manufacturing. Finally, the stock appears to have a potential upside of +21.75%, making it a value opportunity, and, given all of this, in my opinion, the stock is a Strong Buy.

data.title: OPP: The Preferred Shares Are A Good Deal
data.description:
Introduction

I generally like the preferred equity issued by CEFs as there is a mandatory 200% asset coverage ratio rule, which adds an additional layer of safety to an investment. RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund (OPP) is a CEF focusing on debt securities, and I have been quite interested in the CEF’s preferred equity for quite some time.

Waiting for the full-year performance – but I’m not worried from the perspective of a preferred shareholder

I won’t go into much detail on the CEF’s asset portfolio (just over 50% consists of mortgage-backed securities in a pretty equal split of agency and non-agency and you can find the detailed list of securities here), but I am of course interested in the CEF’s financial performance and how well the preferred dividends are covered.

As RiverNorth/DoubleLine still has to report on its full-year financials, I have to go back to its H1 result, shown below. As you can see, the CEF reported a total investment income of $15.5M and $2.9M in expenses, resulting in a net investment income of just over $12.5M.

As you can see above, the CEF needed to spend approximately $2.75M on the preferred dividends (highlighted in yellow), and it goes without saying these preferred dividends were well-covered by the net investment income.

Another important element I’m keeping an eye on is the asset coverage ratio. The CEF needs to have an asset coverage ratio of at least 200% at any time, and looking at the H1 2025 balance sheet, OPP had approximately $345M in assets, and just $7.5M was covered by liabilities. The $120M in preferred equity means the coverage ratio was approximately 281%.

I think the mandatory 200% asset coverage ratio is an important element for a preferred shareholder as the CEF will either have to raise additional equity and/or sell assets should the coverage ratio drop below that 200% while it will also have to suspend distributions on the common shares. While it’s not an absolute protection, it definitely goes a long way to build in some protection. That being said, CEFs will take measures if the coverage ratio comes too close to the limits.

A brief recap of the preferred shares

As mentioned in my previous article, the CEF has two specific series of preferred shares outstanding.

The Series A preferred shares are trading with (OPP.PR.A) as ticker symbol and offer a cumulative dividend of $1.09375 per share per year (payable in 4 equal quarterly tranches of just over $0.27 per quarter), which results in a preferred dividend yield of 4.375% based on the par value of $25/share. These securities can be called from November 15 th this year on.

The Series B preferred shares are trading with (OPP.PR.B) as ticker symbol and are also cumulative in nature. This series was issued in Q2 FY2022 and these preferred shares are paying a preferred dividend of $1.1875 per share (once again payable in four equal quarterly tranches) and can be called from February 15, 2027 on.

Given the current situation on the financial markets I think it is extremely unlikely to see any of both preferred shares being called right now. Although investors get a yield that's north of 6%, the cost of capital for the CEF is the respective 4.375% and the 4.75% which really makes it a very low cost of equity.

As you can see below, the Series A preferred shares issued by the fund are currently trading at $17.13 per share for a current yield of 6.38%.

The Series B are currently trading at $18.37 for an unstripped yield of 6.50%. As both series of preferred shares have the same risk profile (and I don’t think a call risk is a real risk right now), It only makes sense for investors to pick the preferred share that has the highest yield. Right now, that’s the Series B, but the situation could be different in a few weeks or months from now.

Investment thesis

I like preferred equity that was issued by CEFs (in general) as this adds an additional layer of safety to an investment considering the asset coverage ratio has to exceed 200% (or corrective action will be required). Granted, the yield on the preferred equity is substantially lower than what RiverNorth’s common equity is paying (approximately 13%), but the distribution on the common shares has already been cut by almost 50% since 2020 while the share price has also dropped by in excess of 30% compared to five years ago. That's why I think having exposure to the preferred equity makes a little bit more sense for the more conservative investors given the eroding share price of the common shares.

I currently have no long position in the preferred stock of RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund but with a preferred yield of close to 6.5%, the series B preferred shares are getting increasingly interesting. I currently have no intention to initiate any position in the common shares.

data.title: Is Archer Aviation Stock A Sleeper Hit In The Flying Car Wars?
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

It’s no secret that flying car stocks have stayed red-hot this past year, with Archer Aviation (ACHR) and Joby Aviation (JOBY) leading the charge.

I covered Joby recently, arguing it’s carving a unique dual role as a builder and operator of air-taxi networks, effectively combining Uber (UBER)-like ride-hailing economics and Boeing (BA)-level manufacturing.

Though Joby’s my pick among the eVTOL players (electric Vertical Take-Off and Landing), Archer’s worth a calculated bet too, for those who are willing to stomach the risk. Archer’s diversified routes to sales, a powerful global rollout model, and smart capital raises give it room to surprise.

Moreover, a production Midnight landed in Abu Dhabi, and more importantly, these impressive deployments are layered with actual sales on deck. Also, there’s the defense angle that offers a ton of long-term upside potential.

It’s still early in the piece, but its balance sheet is fortified, and with real strategic partners, Archer stock is attractive, trading well below key peers.

Archer vs. Joby: Different Flight Plans for the eVTOL Future

Archer and Joby are in the same boat in the flying car space, but are on very different routes. Joby is going full vertical, looking to design, build, and operate its own fleet.

The play is that it’s going for full control of its stack, from airframe to app. Analysts often call it “Uber meets Boeing,” with Tesla (TSLA)-like first-mover advantage if all things work out.

Archer’s playing a different hand. It’s building and designing eVTOLs, but it’s not quite betting solely on being an airline. Instead, Archer is separating itself with strategic partnerships along with early monetization abroad.

For instance, Archer is working with automaker Stellantis (STLA) in jump-starting high-volume manufacturing, instead of building the capacity entirely in-house.

Also, while Joby’s primary focus is on attaining U.S. certification first, Archer’s goal is to deploy aircraft in the UAE to generate genuine sales before FAA approval.

That strategy is typically what you see with tech companies that beta-test in smaller markets. In doing so, Archer’s CEO, Adam Goldstein, stresses that these UAE flights in extreme heat essentially validate Archer’s tech under harsh conditions.

Also, another edge is that the company is aggressively courting the military market.

Archer is beefing up its potent defense toolkit with two key acquisitions, sharpening both its technology and production.

Earlier this month, it scooped up Overair’s tiltrotor patents and top engineers. That’s basically an IP grab, which not only gives it greater range and speed but keeps the advanced designs out of competitor reach.

Also, Archer bought Mission Critical Composites, which is complete with a 60,000 sq. ft Huntington Beach facility, which targets defense-grade carbon fiber. Hence, Archer now has its own facility for quicker prototyping while securing defense-related projects.

Also, with the Pentagon earmarking $13.4 billion for these systems in its 2025 budget, the addressable market is apparently massive.

However, there’s a caveat that might temper your enthusiasm for Archer’s defense push.

For starters, Archer signed a massive Air Force contract (worth up to $142 million) back in 2022, but that hasn’t yet flowed through as sales.

In fact, as of June 30 this year, the company reported $3.3 million in cash received (deferred), with just one Midnight aircraft delivered under the program.

Nevertheless, the implications are real, and Archer’s differentiated strategy gives it multiple shots on goal.

2025 Milestones: Test Flights, Olympics, and UAE Launch Plans

It has been a very active 2025 for Archer, progressing from sci-fi-esque concept to ‘this is really happening’.

Just this month, its prototype Midnight completed an impressive 55-mile piloted flight (a record) in hardly 31 minutes with an eye-catching 150 mph speed.

For perspective, that’s long enough for regional hops, whereas most would’ve been fine with just short city skips.

Interestingly, United Airlines (UAL) CFO Mike Leskinen was on site, thoroughly impressed by the speed and relatively low-noise profile. For Archer, every test like this takes it up a notch, ticking another box toward FAA certification.

Commercial momentum is just as strong.

Archer just locked in a key role of Official Air Taxi Provider for the 2028 LA Olympics, with vertiports planned at LAX, SoFi, and the Coliseum.

That gives it primetime visibility in the most followed sporting event globally. Top that up with a new White House order endorsing advanced air mobility, and Archer's regulatory path is likely to get a lot less complicated.

Moreover, as the U.S. certification path plays out, Archer is already moving things along overseas.

In July 2025, it delivered its first production Midnight to Abu Dhabi, with breakthrough agreements with Abu Dhabi Aviation in launching routes. These agreements give Archer an edge, as real money soon follows.

Archer expects $20 million to $30 million in sales over 2025–26 from UAE operations, including deals in Indonesia and Ethiopia.

That’s not just cash, but it involves gaining access to operating data, infrastructure testing, and a head start over its competition.

Over the long run, Archer’s looking to target a U.S. launch by 2026, with production ramping to 50+ aircraft in 2027, along with a global fleet in the hundreds by 2030.

Archer Spends Big, but Sits on a Sector-leading Cash Cushion

Archer’s Q2 numbers were far from being a surprise.

As a fast-growing aerospace business that’s not generating any sales, it’s naturally burning through a ton of cash to get airborne.

For perspective, it posted a massive $206 million net loss, wider than last year, while its operational expenses surged $176 million with R&D and manufacturing in overdrive.

However, like I said, such losses aren’t a surprise, and are effectively the cost of pushing six Midnight aircraft through production (three already in final assembly) as it marches toward FAA certification.

The silver lining, though, is that Archer’s balance sheet resembles nothing like a cash-strapped startup.

Following a $850 million raise in Q2, the company wrapped things up with $1.7 billion in liquidity, a fourth consecutive quarterly record, ahead of Joby’s haul.

At the current burn, it boasts a couple of years of runway, a luxury that most EV or aerospace disruptors never had.

On top of that, Archer’s partnerships also help soften the outflow. United and Air Force contracts are covering the testing bill somewhat, while you have Jetex and other infrastructure partners shouldering vertiport buildouts.

Archer guides for a Q3 adjusted EBITDA loss of $110 to $130 million, mostly in line with Q2, indicating that the cash burn is stabilizing.

CEO Adam Goldstein also told its investors that Archer is “executing from a position of strength.”

On the call, he also discussed his dual focus on FAA certification and UAE/global launch programs. He also added that federal backing, from the White House’s executive order, builds a “regulatory moat” for early movers.

Hence, despite the losses mounting, Archer’s fortified war chest and a clear roadmap add substantially to its bull case.

Valuation Discussion

Archer’s stock has been on a wild run, skyrocketing over 250% in late 2024, then another 80% in spring 2025. However, since then, we’ve seen the stock cool off with a 25% to 30% pullback into summer.

For some, that drop mostly looks like a weakness but for investors with a long-term horizon, it may be an excellent opportunity. A big part of that is because of how its commercial pipeline feels a lot more tangible than its current price implies.

With its UAE deployment in place, it’s likely to start generating tens of millions in sales over the next 18–24 months. Throw in its defense contract potential and its robust balance sheet, and you have a deal that’s a lot sweeter than it was during its parabolic run earlier in the year.

From the chart above, we can see that analysts are modeling an absolutely insane revenue ramp over the next couple of years or so.

Consensus goes from basically nil this year to over $100 million by 2026, and then $416 million in 2027, while blasting past the billion-dollar mark in 2028.

Moreover, as those sales stack up, the forward price-to-sales multiple compresses too, from a nosebleed 4,188 times in 2025 to just under 6 times by 2028.

Its valuation has reset, and with volatility known to be part of the package, Archer stock is worth betting on with a cleaner entry point at this point. Also, if the execution stays on track, the valuation gap may not last.

Risks To The Thesis

Let’s be honest, betting on a stock like Archer isn’t exactly for the faint of heart.

The tech is fresh, and it's still pre-revenue, and that situation is unlikely to change anytime soon. Also, a big part of its growth story hinges on Midnight getting certified by 2026, and if it fails, the whole story starts to wobble.

As of now, Archer says the FAA has accepted roughly 15% of its compliance-verification documents, which is encouraging, but there’s still a ton of work left.

Also, the company recently shifted its aft “lifter” props to a four-blade X configuration. That seems like a fix to address potential noise or vibration troubles, but the change itself means more regression testing and could slow down certification.

Flight testing is advancing, with its impressive 55-mile, 31-minute piloted run, but it was a Conventional Takeoff and Landing [CTOL] rather than a full Vertical Takeoff and Landing VTOL mission. Hence, that’s just a partial proxy for air-taxi economics.

Additionally, they’ve got cash, alright, but they’re burning over $100 million a quarter, and any major production hiccups would mean a capital raise, potentially on more testing terms.

Takeaway On Archer Aviation Stock

Archer Aviation stock is one of the handful of eVTOL names that’s turning sci-fi into substance.

There’s a real aircraft involved, some early sales, Olympic exposure, and a defense beachhead, as it continues stacking test-flight wins.

Sure, the flying car space is incredibly volatile, but Archer isn’t exactly selling vapor; it’s building a roadmap.

The latest pullback in many ways could be a gift for long-term backers; hence, for risk-tolerant bulls, it might be the cleanest way to play the flying car future.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Path To $400+ By FY 2026 End
data.description:
Investment thesis

UnitedHealth (UNH) looks like a no-brainer 'Strong Buy' to me because it is a vastly undervalued flagship health care company of the United States. Cost headwinds are highly likely temporary, meaning that the EPS is poised to recover within the next few quarters. Unfavorable developments related to government cost-cutting for healthcare programs like Medicare and Medicaid are also likely to be temporary because demographic trends are favorable for UNH. Since headwinds are temporary, despite being strong, I see the current 34% as a compelling investment opportunity.

Company information

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated is "a health care and well-being company with a mission to help people live healthier lives and help make the health system work better for everyone," according to the company's latest 10-K report.

The company conducts its business via four segments: Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare [including UnitedHealthcare Employer & Individual, UnitedHealthcare Medicare & Retirement and UnitedHealthcare Community & State].

UNH's fiscal year ends on December 31.

Recent developments

First of all, we have to say that the entire Healthcare sector is underperforming the broader U.S. equity market in 2025. This cautious stance is mostly due to the political uncertainty, as President Trump's administration has several initiatives for the sector that might adversely affect the profits of healthcare companies.

Trump's administration strives to optimize U.S. governmental spending, and since the national healthcare expenditure [NHE] represents more than 17% of the country's GDP [the largest in the world], it is an apparent candidate for optimization. Within NHE, Medicare and Medicaid are among the largest items. Financial performance of UnitedHealth is heavily dependent on governmental spending on Medicare and Medicaid because these subsegments collectively generated 40% of the company's total revenue in FY2024.

Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act [OBBBA], which became law on July 4, cuts more than $1 trillion in Medicaid funding over the next several years. This is an apparent short-term headwind for UNH, and the company's revenue growth is expected to decelerate, notably starting from next fiscal year. I am saying that it is highly likely a temporary headwind because secular trends look favorable for UnitedHealth.

First, the population of the United States is growing. According to the Congressional Budget Office, the U.S. population is expected to increase from 350 million in 2025 to 372 million in 2055.

Second, the U.S. population is aging quite rapidly. As the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis suggests, the share of U.S. population ages 65 and above increased from 14% to almost 18% over the last decade. This percentage is expected to grow further and reach 20.4% by 2040. In absolute terms, the number of older [ages 65 and above] Americans is expected to reach 80 million individuals by 2040 compared to 54 million in 2020. Since elderly people are usually the most demanding in health care, this means a significant expansion of UNH's addressable market.

From the cost-side perspective, this year's EPS pullback is painful but also does not look secular. According to the Q2 earnings call, the company faces increased costs this year due to increased medical utilization. This is explained by patients returning for care that was deferred during the pandemic. This deferred effect might last for the next 3-4 quarters, in my opinion. I think so because the pandemic was quite long. Nevertheless, the spike is temporary. Therefore, I tend to agree with Wall Street analysts forecasting a quite rapid recovery of the company's EPS after the expected FY2025 nosedive.

I think that UnitedHealth's balance sheet is healthy, please excuse my redundancy. Liquidity ratios are on a decent level, and there is a $32 billion cash pile. Out of the $79 billion total debt, only $5 billion is short-term. Even the total debt amount is significantly lower than UNH's $275 billion market cap. Therefore, I think that UNH is well-equipped to endure headwinds even if they last for a couple of years, not quarters. Last but not least, even despite a sharp projected FY2025 EPS dip, it is still widely above zero. UnitedHealth's financial strength is underscored by the recent confirmation of its investment-grade credit rating by reputable agency Fitch.

Even if the uncertainty around government-backed programs persists, UNH looks hedged quite well. Besides UNH's firm financial positioning and favorable demographic, let's also keep in mind that the company is not only about capitalizing on Medicare or Medicaid. The business mix is solid and well-diversified. For example, the company's Optum segment, which provides data and health management services, is thriving. This segment's revenue grew from around $183 billion in FY2022 to $253 billion in FY2024, which was a massive 38% increase over just two years. During the first half of FY2025, the segment also demonstrated solid growth. I think that UNH's scale and firm financial position make the company well positioned to develop this thriving business further.

Additionally, UNH's massive footprint in healthcare and loads of data it has accumulated position the company well to absorb positive effects of the ongoing digital revolution. The healthcare analytics market is a thriving niche, which is expected to show a 21.1% CAGR between 2024 and 2030. The "Optum Insight" subsegment is the direct beneficiary of this favorable secular trend. Despite being relatively small compared to UNH's scale, it has recently passed its profitability inflection point, and its operating profit is soaring. The sub-segment's Q2 2025 operating margin expanded to almost 21% from 12% on a YoY basis. Given strong tailwinds in the healthcare analytics market, I see a bright future for this sub-segment.

I also consider this year's management shakeup as a positive sign. Stephen Hemsley was appointed as UNH's CEO again in May 2025. His previous tenure [2006-2017] was extremely successful, as UNH's market cap has approximately tripled during this period. The good part is that Stephen Hemsley already has vast experience managing UNH during crises because he was in charge during the Great Recession.

Valuation

UNH has lost almost 50% of its value over the last twelve months and recorded -40% year-to-date. This performance approximately mirrors the company's projected FY2025 EPS dip.

As I usually say, comparing multiples of giants and growth stars to the sector median values does not make much sense. For the largest [by far] health care provider in the U.S., there are actually now peers. A company with TTM revenue of $422 billion [as twice as high as GDP of my oil-rich motherland Kazakhstan] is in its own private league. Therefore, it is much better to compare current multiples with historical averages.

UNH is currently cheaper than its historical metrics across the board. The TTM Price/Cash Flow ratio is slightly sub-10 while the historical average is almost 20, meaning a 50%+ undervaluation gap. Forward P/E ratios are also extremely low, taking into account that a relatively rapid recovery of UNH's EPS is highly likely.

The historical average [5Y] TTM P/E ratio of UNH is 21.47. An average estimate of 26 Wall Street analysts for the FY2026 EPS is $18.89. The sample of reputable opinions looks reliable, meaning that this EPS forecast can be multiplied by the historical average P/E to calculate the target price for the next sixteen months, targeting the end of FY2026. That said, $18.89 multiplied by 21.47 equals ~$406. This value is 34% higher compared to the last close of ~$303. The upside potential looks compelling, no doubt.

The fact that Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway acquired stake in UNH underscores the fact that valuation is compelling and headwinds are temporary. Moreover, we all know that Warren Buffett is a long-term investor, which means that he is not just trying to time the market but believes in long-term potential of UNH.

Risks to consider

I have to be honest with my readers and want to say that I bought UNH notably cheaper than it currently is, I just didn't have spare time to share an article with you. What I am trying to say is that the stock already demonstrated a notable rebound over short period, which made the stock closer to overbought levels. Moreover, slightly above $300 might look like an attractive level for traders who bought at $240-$250 to realize their profits. Therefore, a near term temporary dip is possible.

The fact that I mentioned as a potential secular catalyst, the rapidly aging U.S. population, might be offset by additional Medicare or Medicaid budget cuts. I consider such a scenario highly unlikely because additional cuts might adversely affect the approval rating of Trump, which is already quite low. Nevertheless, I have to say it because if the risk materializes, UNH's long-term revenue growth prospects might deteriorate further.

Bottom line

All in all, UNH is a "strong buy." Secular trends are favorable, while it is highly likely that all the risks and uncertainties regarding Medicare and Medicaid funding are already priced in. The spike in costs looks temporary, meaning that the EPS is poised to show signs of recovery in FY2026. Therefore, current valuation is dirt cheap, even after the recent sharp rally.

data.title: Salesforce, Broadcom Set To Report Earnings As Markets Await Jobs Report
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

The stock market opened in the red on Friday as the July PCE report came in line, while Marvell's results weighed on Nasdaq. Wall Street's major averages finished higher on Thursday as investors weighed a wave of economic data and Nvidia's latest earnings, all as the S&P 500 surpassed 6,500 and marked yet another record close. In more rate cut news, Fed Governor Christopher Waller said Thursday he favors beginning U.S. interest rate cuts next month and anticipates additional cuts over the next three to six months, aiming to "ease monetary policy and move it to a more neutral stance."

As markets wait for the anticipated rate cut announcement next month, investors will be keeping a close eye on a slate of economic events scheduled to be announced next week. Some important economic reports that will be announced next week include Global Manufacturing PMI and Construction Spending on Tuesday and MBA Mortgage Applications, Factory Orders, and JOLTS Job Openings on Wednesday. Meanwhile Thursday will include the release of Challenger Job Cuts, Exports, and Imports, with reports on Nonfarm Payrolls, and Unemployment Rate due to be announced on Friday.
