data.title: Carpenter Technology: The Strong Bottom Line Could Drive Further Gains (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in May, I published an article on specialty metals and alloys manufacturer, Carpenter Technology (CRS), wherein I rated the stock a Hold due to its huge momentum and outperformance, despite noting that the company is ''...clearly benefiting from multi-year margin expansion and robust earnings growth.'' CRS has returned 42.3% in the year-to-date; 68.5% over the past 52 weeks, and 1,017% over the past five years. However, the stock has lagged a bit in the three months since publication, returning 8.3% vs. 11.4% by the S&P 500 after CRS reversed the upward trend and shed 12% over the past month. CRS is now fast approaching oversold territory, with the 14-day RSI reading falling to 46.7.

In the May article, I cautioned that CRS’ poor valuation scores could limit near-term gains. To wit, key valuation metrics such as the PE ratio, EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, and Price/Sales are all significantly higher than the sector median. However, the stock’s low PEG (price/earnings-to-growth) ratio of 1.07 vs. 1.38 by the sector suggests that the shares could have significant upside thanks to Carpenter's strong bottom line growth. Last month, Carpenter Technology reported mixed fourth quarter and full year FY 2025 results. Fourth quarter revenue of $755.6M (-5.4% Y/Y) missed by $35.22M while Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.21 beat by $0.15. Carpenter delivered $151.4 million in Q4 adjusted operating income, a record quarterly result, up 21 Y/Y and 10% sequentially. While the quarterly report was mixed, the annual report yields some interesting insights into the health of the company. Two years ago, at Carpenter Technology's Investor Day in May 2023, the company unveiled its Four Year Plan wherein it set a target to hit between $460 million and $500 million in operating income, double its pre-COVID high. Well, the company has been able to achieve that in just two years: Carpenter generated $525.4 million in adjusted operating income in FY 2025, good for a healthy 48% Y/Y increase. What makes this achievement more remarkable is the fact that the company has been able to maintain robust earnings growth at a time when the aerospace supply chain slowed down due to the lingering effects of the pandemic, including widespread labor shortages for skilled workers coupled with limited availability of critical raw materials like aluminum and titanium. Meanwhile, the medical industry was going through a destocking phase. Aerospace & Defense is the company’s largest end-use market, contributing 61.5% to its FY '25 revenues, while Medical is the third largest with a 12.2% slice. Aerospace & Defense posted $1,786 million in the last financial year, representing 15% Y/Y growth, while Medical sales shrank 6% to $351 million.

Carpenter also disaggregates revenue under the Specialty Alloys Operations [SAO] and Performance Engineered Products [PEP] segments. SAO sales increased 5% Y/Y to $2,877 million despite a 11% decrease in the volume of SAO products sold to 186,270 pounds thanks to a favorable product mix. PEP revenues clocked in at $405 million, representing a slight 1% decline from the previous year. Another key milestone: expanding profit margins. The company’s pivotal SAO segment posted operating margins of 30.5% in the fourth quarter, a large increase from 25.2% posted in Q4 FY 24 and 29.1% in the prior quarter. A year ago, Carpenter’s management announced that the company had line of sight to margins of ~30%; Carpenter is now optimistic that its margins will continue to expand further in the coming quarters due to a combination of improvements in productivity, product mix optimization, as well as pricing actions. Carpenter Technology has now recorded 16 straight quarters of margin expansion.

During the earnings call, Carpenter’s management set a new target for adjusted operating income, with $660 million to $700 million expected for FY 2026 (current financial year), good for a 26% to 33% Y/Y increase, and $765 million to $800 million for FY 2027, good for nearly 25% CAGR over the two years. This marks the highest growth clip amongst its industry peers. In the near term, Carpenter has forecast that its bottom line will continue to expand at a fast clip in the current fiscal year, with operating income expected to be materially higher in the second half of FY 26 compared to H1 2026, thanks in large part to a continuation of the aerospace supply chain ramp.

Carpenter Technology finished the fiscal year with $664.4 million in total liquidity, including $315.5 million in cash. The company's leverage ratio remains at historic lows, with the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio at 0.5x and zero near-term debt maturities. Carpenter has a strong free cash flow outlook: the company expects to generate $240 million to $280 million in adjusted free cash flow in fiscal year 2026. For clarity, this FCF guidance is net of the $175 million to $185 million in anticipated capital expenditures related to the company's brownfield capacity expansion project.

Carpenter’s balance sheet reveals prudent capital allocation, with Return on Capital Employed [ROCE] having grown 99% over the past five years despite capital employed staying relatively flat over the period. In other words, the business is generating nearly twice the amount of returns it did five years ago from a similar amount of capital, a sign of improving productivity and efficiencies. Further, the company’s ROCE of 17% is significantly higher than the 9.8% average for the Metals and Mining industry.

As I pointed out in the previous article, Carpenter Technology manufactures highly specialized products, and in many cases it’s the only company capable of making the materials. Here's an excerpt from my previous article:

The company's specialty alloy products for critical end-use applications not only command a significant premium but also provide significant barriers to entry. Carpenter's products are further upstream compared to those by aluminum peers Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and Arsenal AIC Parent LLC. Carpenter has a greater focus on aerospace than both companies, especially after Kaiser's acquired the Warrick rolling mill, but has less exposure to packaging, hence Carpenter's higher margins.

The company’s specialized materials go into a wide array of engine platforms and components, including gearing systems, rings and discs, combustors, bearings, and fuel nozzles. In structural applications, its materials are essential in high-performance parts such as wing components and landing gear, as well as other components that require high strength and corrosion/wear resistance. More importantly, the company participates across multiple platforms and OEMs for new builds and MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Overhaul), avoiding overexposure to one particular platform. The diversity of Carpenter's A&D products positions it for long-term growth thanks to growing demand for air travel. According to a ACI World forecast, passenger air traffic will double to 22.3 billion passengers by 2050, good for 3% CAGR. Carpenter is investing $400 million in its brownfield expansion, expected to add primary and secondary melt capacity. This should accelerate revenue growth in the coming years. Meanwhile, as a producer of specialized metal products, Carpenter Technology is largely immune to the ongoing crash in smelting margins. Processing fees for copper and zinc smelters have turned negative thanks to rapid buildout in China, helping global smelter capacity to expand significantly faster than mine production.

Valuation And Risks

As I pointed out before, CRS' biggest weakness is the steep valuation. CRS has poor valuation scores, with most metrics, including the PE ratio, EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA and Price/Sales much higher than its peers'. Thankfully, the company's above-average profit growth means that the valuation is likely to gradually moderate over time.

Carpenter also faces margin risk. The company has established surcharge mechanisms, structured to recover increases in raw material costs by passing through higher input and raw material costs to customers. The company's surcharge revenue has ranged from 13% to 28% of total revenue over the past years. While this surcharge protects the company’s absolute gross profit dollars, it dilutes gross margins as a percentage of sales quite dramatically. This means that the company can easily miss GM targets as raw material prices fluctuate.

The Trump administration has announced a patchwork of different tariff rates for different countries. This included a 35% tariff on all Canadian goods on top of existing duties, excluding products covered by the existing NAFTA treaty. Thankfully, CEO Tony Thene has revealed that Canadian nickel--Carpenter’s biggest raw material input--is exempt from tariffs. Further, Carpenter's surcharge mechanism should be able to take care of any future tariffs.

The Bottom Line

CRS’ diverse and premium aerospace and defense products designed to meet complex customer specifications have been driving robust earnings growth and multi-year margin expansion. The company has guided for robust bottom line growth over the next three financial years, giving the shares room to grow into their steep valuation. The company’s improving ROCE shows that it’s allocating capital efficiently, and its brownfield expansion is likely to accelerate revenue growth in the coming years.

This company ticks all the right boxes for long-term capital growth. I now rate CRS a buy for long-term investors.

data.title: Zscaler Q4 Earnings Preview: Rising On CrowdStrike's Coattails
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

At the start of 2025, I said that Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS) won’t remain cheap for too long.

Since then, I reiterated my “buy” rating on the stock two more times, once in March and then again in June.

Although the stock is down 8% since my last “buy” rating, it is up over 50% since the beginning of the year, beating the S&P 500 by over 5x during this period of time.

The reason I remained bullish on Zscaler is that the company has been able to consistently sustain its enterprise momentum while expanding its platform capabilities to capture market share in a fragmented cybersecurity landscape with Zero Trust Everywhere, Data Security, and Agentic Operations.

Plus, in order to stay ahead of potential enterprise budget cuts in an uncertain macroeconomic environment, Zscaler also launched its new purchasing program called Z-Flex, which would enable customers to flexibly scale the adoption of modules on the platform with simplified procurement and budget predictability.

Zscaler is slated to report its Q4 FY25 earnings on 2nd September after market close. As existing investors in the company, we are well aware that one of the primary themes in the cybersecurity landscape is vendor consolidation, especially as organizations get ready to deploy AI at scale.

At the end of July, we saw Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) agreeing to acquire CyberArk (NASDAQ:CYBR) to accelerate the company’s platform strategy by offering Identity Security as a new core platform.

Last week, we also heard from CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD), which saw its revenue and earnings growth accelerate from “AI necessitated demand for the Falcon platform," thanks to its Falcon Flex subscription program.

In case you are unaware, CrowdStrike and Zscaler have also been strengthening their strategic partnership to offer fully comprehensive cybersecurity solutions as well.

If we take CrowdStrike’s Q2 as a proxy for Zscaler, we believe we should continue to see momentum in enterprise adoption of the company’s Zero Trust Everywhere, Data Security, and Agentic Operations solutions, along with growing traction in their Z-Flex subscription program.

In this post, I have outlined what I will be watching out for in Zscaler’s Q4 earnings report and have reiterated my “buy” rating with a $373 per share price target.

A quick review of Zscaler’s Q3 FY25 earnings

In the previous Q3 FY25 quarter, Zscaler beat both its revenue and earnings estimates, with the former growing 23% YoY at $678M and the non-GAAP operating margin remaining flat year-over-year at 22%.

During the quarter, momentum in enterprise customers spending $100K+ and $1M+ ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) grew at a 19% and 29% CAGR (compounded annual growth rate) over the last two years. This is the case as Zscaler is increasingly focused on expanding their capabilities with Zero Trust Everywhere (which is built on the Zscaler Zero Trust Exchange Platform), where the Zero Trust security goes beyond users and into cloud workloads as well as for branches, enabling deeper adoption of their platform, especially as CXOs are looking to eliminate legacy security stacks that include VPNs and SD-WANs.

Not only that, but Zscaler is also strengthening its Data Security platform and rapidly expanding its Agentic Operations category across ITOps and SecOps, with strong booking growth in its ZDX Copilot offering in the last quarter.

As Zscaler broadens its platform to include workload protection, segmentation, data security, and security operations, it also launched a new purchasing program called Z-Flex, which allows customers to flexibly scale their adoption of the platform based on agreed pricing. This is very similar to CrowdStrike’s Falcon Flex subscription program, which has been hugely successful in driving enterprise adoption so far.

In my previous post, I discussed that the traction for Z-Flex has been promising so far, while the management was also confident that revenue contribution from Z-Flex will "meaningfully grow in the next fiscal year”.

This is what I am looking for in Q4, taking CrowdStrike as a proxy.

Last week, CrowdStrike reported its Q2 earnings, where it managed to beat and accelerate revenue during the quarter, growing at 21.3% YoY, while diluted earnings per share came in at $0.93, 12% higher than management’s expectation.

During the quarter, CrowdStrike CEO George Kurtz attributed the reacceleration in Q2 to the "AI-necessitated demand for the Falcon platform," where they now expect 40% YoY growth in net new ARR for the back half of the fiscal year, driven by strong customer retention, expanding platform adoption, and AI-driven demand.

Plus, CrowdStrike has now surpassed 1000 customers who have adopted their Flex subscription with an average deal size that is greater than $1M. Moreover, CrowdStrike added 220 new Flex customers during Q2 and saw over 100 customers that “re-flexed,” a.k.a., expanded their spend on the platform.

On that note, I will point out that Zscaler and CrowdStrike have an existing strategic partnership, which was strengthened even more with the recent acquisition of Red Canary by Zscaler. As per their expanded partnership, the collaboration between these two companies will deliver a more comprehensive cybersecurity solution that integrates CrowdStrike’s Falcon platform with Zscaler’s Zero Trust Exchange and Red Canary’s Managed Detection and Response capabilities.

Therefore, taking the strength in CrowdStrike’s Q2 earnings into account, here’s what I will be looking for in Zscaler’s Q4 FY25 earnings.

First, consensus estimates currently point to Zscaler growing its Q4 revenues by 19.27% YoY. While this would mark a sequential slowdown in revenue, the company would need to beat estimates by 330 basis points in order to maintain a similar growth rate as the previous quarter.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings, it is expected to decline close to 9% YoY to $0.8 per share. However, if we look ahead, it is expected to get better from here onwards.

Having said that, I believe that investors will be looking for momentum in the strength of enterprise customers. In other words, if Zscaler can continue to maintain its CAGR from its $100K+ and $1M+ ARR customers, it would be a testament that their product innovation with Zero Trust Everywhere, as well as with Data Security and Agentic Operations, is resonating with their customers.

In Q3, 59% of customers who bought Zero Trust Branch (as part of Zero Trust Everywhere) were new logo customers, and the management was bullish that it would be a significant growth driver in FY26. Similarly, the company also saw larger deal sizes for Zero Trust Cloud, securing a larger number of workloads, with the total number of Zero Trust Everywhere enterprises growing 60% quarter-over-quarter in the last quarter.

Finally, given the success of CrowdStrike’s Falcon Flex subscription program, investors will be looking for similar results with Z-Flex in driving growing adoption of the Zscaler Zero Trust Exchange platform.

With that said, while investors will be pleased with Zscaler beating its top and bottom line estimates in Q4, all eyes will be on forward guidance for FY26.

At the moment, consensus estimates are pointing to Zscaler’s revenue growing at 20.45% YoY in FY26, which would be a deceleration compared to the expected 22.75% YoY growth rate in FY25.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings estimates, Zscaler is expected to see its earnings per share reaccelerate from next year onwards and further improve in the following years. When it comes to management’s expectation for non-GAAP operating margin, they project it at 22%. With earnings per share estimates expected to reaccelerate from FY26 onwards, we can expect non-GAAP operating margin to move higher, especially as the company gains operating leverage from enterprise momentum.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will take consensus estimates into account over the next five years for revenue growth, which points to Zscaler growing in the 20%+ range to $5.7B by FY29. Note that this is slightly lower than CrowdStrike’s and Cloudflare’s (NYSE:NET) forward revenue projections. Meanwhile, both Zscaler and Cloudflare compete with each other on network security with their respective Zero Trust and SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) solutions. Given that both Zscaler and Cloudflare are roughly the same size, should Zscaler demonstrate its forward revenue growth to meaningfully match up to Cloudflare’s mid-twenties range over the coming years, it would present a significant rerating opportunity for the stock. For now, we will stick to forward consensus estimates for our own risk management purposes.

Meanwhile, when it comes to profitability, I continue to believe that Zscaler should see its non-GAAP operating margin expand from 22% in FY25 to 25% in FY29, especially with a growing contribution of larger deal sizes and momentum from its Z-Flex subscription service.

This should translate to Zscaler generating $1.4B in non-GAAP operating income by FY29, which is equivalent to a present value of $1.05B when discounted at 8%.

Taking a forward price-to-earnings multiple of 3 times the average S&P 500 multiple, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $373 per share, which remains unchanged from my previous post and represents an upside of 35% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

With the cybersecurity industry facing increasing pressures from vendor consolidation, I believe CrowdStrike’s Q2 earnings paint a promising picture for Zscaler’s upcoming Q4 earnings as well. As an existing investor, Zscaler needs to demonstrate momentum in its $100K+ and $1M+ ARR customers, as it has been doubling down on expanding its platform capabilities and extending its strategic partnership with CrowdStrike. Most importantly, investors will be paying close attention to forward guidance. Should management guide revenue in FY26 to grow at par or higher than FY25, we can expect a significant rerating higher.

data.title: Industry-Leading Margins And Record Cash Flow Position Newmont For Long-Term Value Creation
data.description:
Newmont Corporation (NEM) has shown incredible strength in 2025, as its share price is up an astounding 85% year-to-date in an exceptionally strong gold market environment. Currently positioned in a long-term bullish channel that has developed since 2015, the world's largest gold producer finds itself at a meaningful technical level after its recent all-time high of $86.37. With current resistance at $71 creating healthy consolidation, Newmont appears ready for a corrective pullback that should be a fantastic buying opportunity for it to eventually move higher towards $120-$140.

My analysis suggests that Newmont is a company operating at the highest levels of operational and financial efficiency, with Q2 2025 results revealing record quarterly free cash flow of $1.7 billion and earnings per share of $1.43 that beat consensus by a remarkable 25%. Combined with profitability metrics that include 30.5% net income margin and 20.3% return on equity, Newmont is performing very well within the mining industry. While technical analysis indicates minor consolidation toward the $50-$60 price remains probable over the near term, the longer-term outlook remains definitively bullish based on Seeking Alpha Quant's Strong Buy rating and Newmont's strategic initiative around portfolio optimization.

Business Update: Record Performance Drives Operational Excellence

Newmont posted outstanding Q2 2025 results, further cementing itself as the undisputed market leader in the gold mining space. The company reported normalized earnings per share of $1.43, a stunning 25.44% beat compared to analyst expectations, and ably reflected the results of much systematic divestiture program and an ability to manage an efficient operation. Revenue came in at $5.32 billion, a 9.92% beat to expectation, reflecting efficient management but with a favorable precious metals environment.

The quarter's highlight was a record quarterly free cash flow of $1.7 billion, a 42% increase over the prior quarter, and a record for Newmont. This remarkable cash generation capability speaks to the company's world-class portfolio of assets, along with management's disciplined yet opportunistic approach to capital allocation. Operating cash flow was $2.4 billion, representing significant coverage for capital investments and returns to shareholders and being able to maintain financial flexibility for opportunities.

Even with the expected impact of asset sales, production metrics remained strong. Newmont produced 1.5 million attributable gold ounces and 36,000 tonnes of copper in the quarter. The small decline in production from P-rate quarters to this quarter was fully anticipated and is consistent with the strategic divestiture program successfully undertaken by Newmont, which has transitioned towards higher-margin, longer-life assets. Average realized gold price of $3,320 per ounce, up from $2,944 in the previous quarter of an increase of $376 per ounce, provided a substantial portion of the very strong financial performance.

Newmont stuck to its full-year 2025 guidance, despite short-term operational challenges, for 5.6 million ounces of gold production at all-in sustaining costs of $1,630 per ounce. The company has the confidence of achieving its guidance, which illustrates the fundamental strength of its portfolio, as well as the operational excellence in several jurisdictions.

Strategic Portfolio Transformation: Maximizing Value Through Focus

Newmont’s transformational divestiture strategy has completely changed the company’s asset base, making it a more concentrated, higher-margin company. The company expects to receive $3.0 billion in after-tax cash proceeds from its 2025 divestiture strategy and approximately $2.5 billion received from divestitures and $470 million from equity monetization activities. Newmont's exit of lower-margin operations has been strategically re-focused to Tier 1 assets with better economics, while allowing management to focus capital and management bandwidth on these higher-margin assets.

Newmont's divestiture program created significant value via equity positions, with Greatland Resources and Discovery Silver producing 230% and 200% returns, respectively. Focusing on divestitures provided Newmont with the ability to create a more resilient business model based upon Tier 1 assets like Boddington, Ahafo, and Nevada Gold Mines joint ventures that have large reserves, long mine lives, and competitive cost structures. In addition to divestitures, Newmont is focused on high-return, low-risk growth, as evidenced by Ahafo North in Ghana, which is expected to provide first gold production in Q4 2025 using Newmont's existing infrastructure and operational scale.

Technical Analysis: Bullish Channel Intact Despite Near-Term Consolidation Signals

Newmont has one of the best long-term setups in the precious metals space on its technical features alone. The stock has been in a solid upward channel since late 2015, just to show a short false breakout in early 2024 before quickly resolving it on the upside. This long-term structural pattern is still on, and continuing to offer strong support and resistances to base decisions against.

The stock just reached an all-time high of $86.37, which confirms significant technical resistance. The stock has consolidated from there, and is now at technical resistance at $71, which is coincidentally also the area above a strong advance where people take profits. A look at the technical pattern suggests a correction will follow soon, and immediate support seems more likely to emerge somewhere in the $50-$60 area, which follows Fibonacci retracement levels (23.6%-38.2%) and diagonal trend lines. This price area is also a prior resistance level, which may now become support, and is showing good, healthy consolidation after a long advance.

The analysis supports a bullish conclusion. With the moving averages (50-, 100-, and 200-week), a golden cross (50-day crosses above 200-day), and the stochastic indicator confirming, it is clear we are in very extreme overbought conditions; we have a recent high with bearish divergence, and I can see that price will either consolidate or correct to the downside in the near term. This is historically where corrections occur, and these levels will present phenomenal entry opportunities for longer-term positions. If this is the breakout scenario, we could have a price target of $120-$140, I would very much like to see a correction back to the support level of $50-$60; that would be an excellent tactical entry opportunity.

Financial Excellence: Industry-Leading Margins Drive Superior Returns

Newmont's financial figures show great operating efficiency and profitability that make it competitive with the rest of the mining industry. Net income margin was 30.49% for the trailing twelve months and considerably ahead of key competitors such as Barrick Gold (B) at about 10%, Agnico Eagle (AEM) at 20%, and Franco-Nevada (FNV) at 25%, showing Newmont's ability to convert revenue to profit at the bottom line. The reported margin is affected by operational improvement and favorable commodity prices, but more importantly, shows sustainability in turning revenue to profit during changing market conditions.

Newmont is a more capital-efficient company, demonstrates uneven capital efficiency with a 20.26% Return on Equity [ROE], quite a bit better than key competitors Barrick Gold (11.41%), Franco-Nevada (12.61%), and very close to Agnico Eagle’s (13.92%). ROE represents a strong value creation for investors, with an emphasis on higher return investments. Similarly, Newmont's Return on Assets [ROA] is also greater than the sector average at 11.38%, portraying the efficiency of owning and not owning only fees after the recent portfolio transformation. Financially, Newmont has very little leverage; a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 0.1x and considerable financial flexibility: $6.2 billion in cash and equivalents and total liquidity of $10.2 billion, including credit, for growth opportunities, but with caution after its portfolio transformation.

The features of cash flow production continue to be the backbone of Newmont's investment thesis: Newmont has generated $8.57B in operating cash flow on a trailing basis, far above capital needs and returns to shareholders. Newmont has a free cash flow margin of 44.52%. Newmont significantly exceeds peer averages with its FCF margin; Barrick Gold has an approximate 15% FCF margin , Agnico Eagle has 25% , and Franco-Nevada has 35% , indicating the quality and durability of Newmont's cash flow production across commodity cycles.

The management of working capital suggests effective operations, while the 9.6% decline year-over-year represents timing changes in accounts payable and in inventory management caused by divestiture program changes. The measure articulated above does not reflect operational challenges; rather, it reflects the timing adjustments that must occur for any actions taken in connection with various portfolio optimization considerations.

Intrinsic Valuation Analysis: Fair Value Indicates Significant Upside Potential

For the DCF calculation, I have made the following assumptions:

  • Annual Free Cash Flow (FCF) growth of 8% over the next five years
  • A terminal growth rate of 3% from year six onwards
  • A 10% discount rate
  • Annualized FCF base of $6.8 billion (using Q2 2025 record quarterly FCF of $1.7 billion)

Newmont's proven ability to continually increase cash flow via portfolio optimization, development of tier one assets, and high gold prices allows for 8% free cash flow [FCF] forecasted growth in years 6 through 10. Recent quarters have shown remarkable FCF growth (42% quarter-over-quarter in Q2 2025), but I selected a below peak growth rate to conservatively account for gold price volatility, asset sales, and ultimately a return to earth after record operational accomplishments. This places the model's growth rate in line with sector forecasts, but below the sharp short-term spikes in cash flow observed in recent quarters, allowing for continued. realism.

The 3% terminal growth rate is consistent with above-average inflation forecasts and global GDP trends. As is standard with mining DCF models. I think long-term growth of three percent is a balanced and reasonable expectation of moderate growth with conservative prudence. It is a requirement to ensure Newmont's valuation does not over-emphasize perpetual growth but stays above the normal rate of shrinkage or stagnation from well-established mining companies.

A 10% discount rate is commonplace within the large, diversified, globally operating mining companies. This value accounts for the commodity cycle, geopolitical risk, and volatility in the sector, and is consistent with other benchmark values of 8-10% for gold and base metal producers. Given Newmont's stable jurisdiction, large reserves, and low leverage, a 10% rate is reasonable, not aggressive, and not small with regard to risk-adjusted expectations, which are consistent with expectations in the current environment.

The valuation takes a two-stage approach, and the summary of the calculations is as follows:

Year Projected FCF (USD M) Discount Factor Discounted FCF (USD M)
1 6,800 0.91 6,188
2 7,344 0.83 6,092
3 7,932 0.75 5,949
4 8,566 0.68 5,830
5 9,251 0.62 5,736
Terminal 147,693 0.62 91,570

The resulting estimated enterprise value is $121,365 million. Net debt was subtracted, and dividing by the total number of shares yielded an estimated fair value of approximately $147 per share.

Based on different valuation methodologies, results:

  • DCF valuation: $147 per share (again, see the above assumptions).
  • Sector multiple-based valuation using averages for the P/E of 17.12x and with an approximate EPS of $5.72 = $98 per share
  • FCF yield method, targeting a 6% yield = $143 per share

The $125-$130 range is provided as a reasonable midpoint between the highest output (DCF) and the weighted average amounts from the multiples and FCF yield approaches. It has been adjusted for market/contextual and technical price targets - a reasonable place between conservative assumptions and existing sector volatility. I justify this range through the similar convergence of our three main valuation methods, which were different from each other (multiples, DCF, and CF yield), but explicitly value the stock at about the same amount between $125 and $130.

Additionally, it is important to note that our fundamental fair value estimate is consistent with the technical price targets mentioned earlier. Technical analysis states a rally in stock could happen, taking it to the $120-$140 target range. Based on our intrinsic valuation, we would suggest we could project to the upper end of that technical target zone. When fundamentally sound fundamentals obviously trade at a steep discount to the reasonable fair value range and the technical setup leans higher, it also creates a very compelling risk-adjusted opportunity for long-term investors.

Exceptional Shareholder Returns: $6 Billion Capital Return Program

Newmont has positioned itself as the gold mining sector's preeminent company for shareholder capital returns, having put in place one of the most aggressive and well-funded return programs of any mining company. In July 2025, Newmont's board of directors approved another $3.0 billion share repurchase program, bringing the total repurchase authorization amount to $6.0 billion. This shows management is confident about the business and value-creating opportunities.

The performance of the company's capital returns program has been nothing short of remarkable, with Newmont returning more than $1 billion to shareholders in dividends and share buybacks since our last earnings announcement. For a company to return this amount of capital implies they are generating considerable free cash flow and have a solid balance sheet. The share buyback program allows management to retain flexibility in terms of when to return capital in order to maximize shareholders' value when they perceive the company is undervalued.

For the income investor, the dividend policy continues to look attractive. Newmont has maintained its quarterly dividend payment of $0.25 per share. For the income investor, the dividend policy looks bright. Newmont continued to pay its quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share, which provides a current yield of 1.41%. While the yield may not appear exciting in comparison with other dividend stocks, its sustainability is exceptional since Newmont has a very favorable payout ratio of about +20% and very strong cash generation from operations. Furthermore, the dividend has held up quite well through all sorts of commodity cycles, resulting in steady income-generating power when needed.

Newmont's whole capital return strategy is predicated by attractive valuation metrics and growth potential. Management prefers buybacks to acquisitions in this market and is prioritizing organic growth, productivity, and operational excellence. For shareholders, this action would result in immediate returns through dividends and capital returns via a lower share count above and beyond the impact of overall operational improvements. Newmont's cash generation capabilities are robust, and this gives the company the ability to make sustainable returns even under adverse commodity price conditions.

Growth Projects and Operational Excellence: Building Tomorrow's Production

Newmont has positioned its portfolio for success by targeting high-return, lower-risk development projects that utilize existing assets and operations experience. Ahafo North is Newmont's flagship project, and the Ahafo North deposit has proven reserves, strong geology, existing assets, and has been catalysts for associated neighboring project completion by local teams. Ahafo North expects to first produce gold in Q4 2025; the project is on schedule and within budget. Newmont's project expansion at Ahafo North is imperative for it to maintain flexibility, allow for synergies, and capacity, and will benefit from being within walking distance of its established operations, reliable local workforce, and proven capabilities.

Despite the ups and downs of operating conditions, the Red Chris operation in British Colombia represents long-term growth potential through development opportunity via block cave. Newmont has 70% interest in this copper-gold operation that provides exposure to both base and precious metals markets. A potential block cave development would unlock substantial additional reserves and establish Red Chris as a multi-decade producer with scale.

Newmont is progressing with its Operational Excellence initiatives across the portfolio with a focus on improvements in safety, cost efficiencies, and enhancements in productivity. The "Always Safe" program directly relates safety improvements to a value metric that encourages operational improvements and cost management that eventually lead to industry-leading profitability and the all-important sustainable competitive advantage. The company's overarching technology and innovation focus has resulted in operational efficiencies achieved through, among other things, automation, data analytics, and process optimizations. Newmont wants to enhance its operational focus so that it becomes better in an increasingly technology-driven industry and sees these investments as addressing not only safety but improving productivity.

Valuation Analysis: Attractive Metrics Support Growth Premium

Even with a substantial year-to-date price appreciation, Newmont is still enticingly valued compared to potential growth opportunities and peer comparisons. The forward P/E of 13.27x is less than the sector median of 17.06x, which we see as indicative of the fact that the market is still coming to terms with the company's operational improvements moving forward. Newmont's better measures of profitability and growth profile has also widened the valuation gap.

Newmont's valuation metrics demonstrate strong relative operational performance and market position versus its primary peers Barrick Gold, Franco-Nevada, and Agnico Eagle. Although Newmont has a 13.27x forward P/E that trades at a discount versus Agnico Eagle at 19.9x and Franco-Nevada at 52.3x and a modest premium to Barrick's 15.5x due to better profitability metrics.

The peer analysis indicated Newmont's leadership is not only operationally excellent with key performance indicators, but also qualitatively superior. In terms of net margins, for example, Newmont's 30.5% significantly exceeds Barrick's estimated 10% and Agnico Eagle's 20%, and is approaching the royalty-supported 25% margins of Franco-Nevada. But more importantly, Newmont's data shows robust free cash flow, and margins of 44.5% demonstrate even stronger performance compared to peers. Barrick has approximately 15%, Agnico Eagle at 25%, and Franco-Nevada at 35% free cash flow margins, though they are better than Barrick and Agnico Eagle.

In terms of capital efficiency, Newmont has a 20.3% ROE that is at the top of its peer group versus Barrick at 8% ROE, Franco-Nevada estimated at 15% ROE, and Agnico Eagle at 18%. Newmont's capital efficiency, scale, and breadth of its asset base support a higher value-public market valuation than its peer group, and there will be potential for multiple expansions as the market begins to appreciate the company's upgraded operating profile following its divestiture program.

Seeking Alpha Quant supports a balanced view of valuation, when utilizing algorithms, it assigned Newmont a Strong Buy rating, while also indicating stretched valuation with some metrics. The C rating for valuation indicates premium pricing at the current time, while the A+ profitability and A- momentum ratings indicate management is executing operationally well and the sector is responding favorably in the market. Most significantly, the A+ revisions rating indicates increased analyst confidence, Newmont had 15 increasing earnings revisions in the last three months and no decreasing earnings revisions.

At a price-to-cash flow ratio of 9.06x, Newmont's valuation relative to sector averages and historical valuation multiples appears suitable, thus the valuation is reasonable, especially given the quality of cash generated by the company. In relation to the sustainability of cash flow at current costing levels and the potential for improvement, the valuation at these multiples represents solid value for cash flow investors.

Market Environment: Gold's Structural Bull Case Supports Long-Term Outlook

The fundamental backdrop for gold and gold mining equities remains very supportive, with a number of structural issues aligning themselves, creating fundamental aspects that are very supportive for investing in precious metals. The gold price has risen around 27% year to date, of which geopolitical uncertainty, negative trade relations, and diversification from traditional currency reserves have all contributed to longer-term demand increases.

Central banks continue to produce supporting evidence as they add gold at elevated levels, so far over 900+ tonnes per year for the last two years by the world central banks, while central banks are de-risking by divestment from currency reserves. Institutions will continue to be an impactful structural support for gold prices and likely will not change any time soon as the world continues on with its monetary and political crises.

Inflationary pressures and worries about currency depreciation have also continued to bolster gold's fundamental characteristics as a store of value and portfolio diversifier. While many of the world's central banks are trying to deal with inflation, the enduring inflationary pressure, along with the enormous fiscal deficits that are being created by the large economies of the world, continues to present an environment where gold still serves its role as an inflation hedge and alternative currency to fiat currencies.

The investment demand driver is particularly robust, with inflows into exchange-traded funds totaling approximately 120 tonnes in Q2 2025 alone. Institutional investors are also growing their gold allocations as part of a broader diversification strategy in their portfolios, and based on their views that traditional correlations among assets break down during stressful developments in market conditions.

Risk Factors: Operational and Market Considerations

  • Newmont's investment outlook is related to industry-specific risks. Commodity price fluctuations can directly affect performance, cash flow, and financial multiples.
  • There are risks relating to mining risks, including (e.g., safety, equipment, geological risk), there are risks that will exist for Newmont, but with its diversified portfolio and good safety record, these risks are less severe.
  • Political and regulatory risk could impact operations both domestically and abroad, impacting potential costs or challenges. However, as a globally diversified company, this does lessen some of that potential exposure.
  • With the changing environment and social landscape of public stakeholders, operational flexibility and project timelines could ride on the social goodwill of the community; that being said, the company does have established and formal programs to help mitigate this risk.
  • For non-U.S.-based operations, the conversion of currency fluctuations into financial results may present a potential challenge; while the company does utilize some natural hedging, a robust U.S. dollar could restrict international revenue or increase local costs to operate.

Investment Thesis: Strategic Entry Opportunity at Technical Inflection Point

Investment in Newmont Corporation represents a strong risk/reward for investors in precious metals with great operating performance, structural market advantages, and attractive technical positioning. Newmont has become a more focused and higher-margin player, with leading returns and defensible cash flow from purposeful divestitures to become even more leaner.

Newmont has a lot going for it, including being the largest gold producer in the world, Tier 1 assets, a very strong operating track record, and a solid balance sheet. I perceive record cash flow to the company in the foreseeable future affords tremendous flexibility to support growth and shareable returns while prudently maintaining financial strength. Additionally, the long-term pricing outlook is bullish ($120-$140), with a reasonably solid range of support for creating attractive entry points ($50-$60).

The market conditions for gold are now quite structurally supportive - central bank buying has increased, and geopolitical uncertainty feeds the margin relief and structural advantage Newmont has over those competitors still in commodity retail. I think the priorities and process supporting capital allocation (e.g., buyback program and sustainable dividend, etc.) should lead to greater total return potential as Newmont’s cash flow expands, share count diminishes, and hopefully multiples expand.

Conclusion

Newmont Corporation is discussed as an excellent investment opportunity, with operational excellence, financial strength, and a good market position. The year-to-date surge of 85% suggests Newmont is overcoming a difficult gold market with consistent operational results. From a technical standpoint, the current levels at $71 are defined as resistance, and the $50-$60 levels have been established as entry points with long-term channel indications suggesting possible upside toward $120-$140.

The most recent Q2 2025 results indicate a transformation based on divestitures, record free cash flow, and best-in-class profitability, reiterating sustainable differentiation. The company has also authorized a $6 billion share repurchase plan and maintained the dividend policy to provide different paths to value, detailing a positive outlook from management. For investors looking for operational excellence and capital appreciation in a premium precious metals firm, Newmont is an excellent risk/reward proposition supported by Strong Buy and positive analyst revisions.

From the broader gold price standpoint, positive conditions, in conjunction with Newmont's competitive position and overall balance sheet, indicate solid long-term value creating opportunities. As well, current market conditions appear opportune to add or build positions ahead of future anticipated technical price breakouts and operationally focused improvement. Although a longer-term consolidation period could be frustrating for traders, the combination of fundamentals and current technical market conditions offers clear fundamental alignment for strategic and patient investors to position themselves for differentiated performance moving forward.

data.title: Universal Health Realty: FFO May Reach $3.60/Share In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Universal Health Realty (NYSE:UHT) has outperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a gain of ~13%:

I previously covered UHT with a Buy rating back in June 2025, highlighting the company's attractive valuation in light of my $3.45-3.55/share FFO outlook for 2025. Since then, the REIT's Q2 2025 results indicate FFO in 2025 will likely reach the bottom end of my forecast.

Even so, I anticipate a further increase in 2026 FFO to about $3.60/share, principally driven by Fed rate cuts as well as marginally higher revenues. This puts the forward FFO multiple at only 11.3x, which is quite attractive in light of the company's modest leverage.

As such, even after the strong performance so far in 2025, I confirm my Buy rating for UHT.

Company Overview

You can access all UHT results on its website here. Universal Health Realty is a healthcare REIT focused on medical office buildings/clinics (71% of gross real estate asset value) and acute care hospitals (17%), with the portfolio of 76 buildings unchanged so far in 2025:

The company's enterprise value amounts to ~$925 million, of which 60% is funded with common equity, leaving 40% for net debt.

Q2 2025 Results Overview

UHT reported FFO of $0.85/share in Q2 2025, down 5% Y/Y, impacted primarily by higher property taxes. Still, the quarterly dividend of $0.74/share remains well covered, representing a payout of 87%:

Interest expenses were also marginally higher, impacted by the expiration of favorable interest rate swaps. My estimates show that the REIT's current average cost of debt has risen to about 5.1%.

Tailwind from Fed rate cuts

Looking ahead to 2026, the company will benefit from Fed rate cuts on the $190 million in floating rate debt not covered by interest rate swaps:

Fed funds futures prices indicate that markets expect two, potentially three 0.25% Fed rate cuts by the end of 2025. As such, interest expenses in 2026 should decline by about $1-1.4 million, or about $0.07-0.10/share.

FFO Outlook

I previously estimated that UHT should be able to achieve FFO of $3.45-3.55/share in 2025. With the company achieving an FFO of $1.71/share in the first half of the year, I think it is reasonable to expect a result closer to the bottom end of my anticipated FFO range.

Looking ahead to 2026, the company should benefit from rent indexation in addition to the abovementioned tailwind from Fed rate cuts. Looking at Q2 2025 revenues, sales only grew 0.5% Y/Y, with a comparable increase in 2026 only boosting FFO by circa $0.04/share.

To conclude, I expect FFO of about $3.45/share in 2025 and an increase to about $3.60/share in 2026. Absent tenant bankruptcies, risks are tilted to the upside as the FFO growth from higher revenue may exceed $0.04/share, while incremental Fed rate cuts may also boost cash flows later in the year.

UHT Valuation

The shares trade at just 11.3x my 2026 FFO estimate of $3.60/share, a very attractive value proposition taking into account the company's conservative leverage, with net debt accounting for only 40% of enterprise value.

I estimate that cumulative cash flows to enterprise value amount to $66.7 million after administrative expenses, representing a corporate market-implied cap rate of 7.2%. If we want to look at the valuation from an acquirer's perspective, we can add back the advisory fees to the REIT's manager, which amount to about $5.6 million annually, taking enterprise-level cash flows to about $72.3 million, or a market-implied cap rate of 7.8%.

All in all, whether we look at the FFO multiple or market-implied cap rate, UHT remains attractively valued relative to large-cap healthcare REITs, likely indicating that the company's recent outperformance relative to VNQ is set to continue in 2026. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for UHT.

Risks

The main risk in the investment case stems from UHT's heavy revenue exposure to Universal Health Services (UHS), which accounts for 40% of UHT's Q2 2025 revenues. The good news here is that UHS is a public company, which allows investors to easily track its operational performance. Case in point, analysts expect both higher earnings and revenues for UHS in 2026, indicating there should be no problem in the company meeting its rent obligations:

The second key risk to consider is that the Fed may cut interest rates more slowly than currently priced in by markets, which would delay the FFO boost for UHT.

Last but not least, while incremental Fed rate cuts are expected in 2026, the effect on 2027 FFO will be more nuanced as a beneficial $80 million swap at 1.58% expires in 2027. As the Fed funds rate at that time is expected to be close to 3%, refinancing the swap will likely largely negate the effect of the 2026 Fed rate cuts.

Conclusion

Universal Health Realty reported FFO of $0.85/share in Q2 2025, down 5% Y/Y, impacted by higher property taxes and an increase in interest expenses. This indicates the company is likely to reach the bottom end of my $3.45–3.55/share FFO outlook for 2025.

Looking ahead to 2026, I estimate that FFO should rise to about $3.60/share, driven primarily by Fed rate cuts later this year as well as higher rental income.

UHT currently trades at just 11.3x my FFO estimate for 2026, representing a market-implied cap rate of ~7.8% (an acquirer's perspective without administrative overhead). This is quite attractive relative to large REITs that make up the majority of VNQ constituents, likely indicating that UHT's recent outperformance is set to continue in 2026, thus confirming a Buy rating on the shares.

Key risks in the investment case include the company's outsized 40% revenue exposure to Universal Health Services, as well as the potential for slower-than-expected Fed rate cuts.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Toast: Short-Term Pains May Test Valuations
data.description:
Toast (TOST) has shown strong operational momentum even in Q2 2025, with both earnings and topline beats. The valuations and share prices have rallied in the past year, acknowledging this momentum. Going ahead, Toast could face some consolidation, or even corrections, in my view, as markets take a breather despite potential supportive rate cuts. Drags from a macro spending cut environment, higher costs for the restaurant industry, and overall competitive and cyclical demand risks will keep valuations under check at current levels. The long-term story is still intact with secular digitization and AI-led innovation tailwinds, but in the short term, Toast's high beta (~1.5 in the past two years and higher in the past year alone) may see more volatile short-term corrections before rewarding long-term investors. I believe given the combination of competing factors, Toast is a hold in the current environment, and any short-term dip could be a buying opportunity for long-term gains.

Strong Operational Metrics

Topline growth continues with a 25% jump in revenues, continuing unabated growth despite concerning macro situations that should have started to impact Toast in Q2 2025. ARR grew by 31% YoY. The topline growth is mostly a result of healthy net new location addition of 8.5k in Q2, up by 24% YoY.

Operational progress was visible beyond numbers, with international expansion continuing to excite. Toast reported a first customer in Australia. A multiyear partnership with American Express should also help drive in more users and help support the robust growth trajectory.

Even guidance was revised upwards with 28-29% growth in gross profits expected now in subscription services and fintech solutions, for the full year 2025 (up from earlier guidance of 25-27%). However, the earnings picture is nuanced, particularly in the short term. The current gross margins are decent at ~25%, but continued growth towards the 30% mark will help support the premium valuations. Payments processing margins are inherently low, which means continued growth in subscription revenues will be required to push the blended gross margins towards more healthy territory. Although not an immediate concern, Q2 2025 gross margins have actually dropped marginally despite a decent ARR increase.

Operating margins, where trending growth is more clearly intact, could be pressured going ahead with sales and R&D expense upticks requiring continued topline growth to sustain margins. In Q2 2025, sales and marketing expenses grew ~22% YoY, somewhat reasonable for the growth levels, but still not at a level where we can say Toast is driving efficiencies there and seeing an improving customer acquisition cost. R&D costs are not as material for Toast as most SaaS companies, but with a thin payments margin, even the flat to stable R&D costs will need topline growth to keep happening at pace, to ensure margins are not impacted.

Currently R&D costs are at ~6% of the revenues and have already been through a period of new product launches like Toast IQ and Toast Go 3, showing they will remain under control even as Toast goes through ongoing innovation cycles to stay relevant in a fiercely competitive and evolving market. So, overall R&D and sales are doing really reasonably so far, but short-term pains in topline growth pressure margins, because further efficiencies in both sales and R&D is difficult in Toast's current growth phase.

Multiple Concerns Add Up

Despite a broadly faultless earnings result where most numbers and guidance show healthy execution, Toast has declined by ~8% since. This is often a sign of valuation fatigue and a more conservative market expectations on the road ahead.

I am fundamentally bullish about Toast, but there are macro concerns I have as well. Toast is heavily exposed to consumer cyclicality, inflation, and tariffs that impact its target customer segment. Slower IT spending is usually more pronounced in the restaurant sector, particularly given their own thin margins and multiple cost heads that need to be managed. Rising labor costs, input cost increases due to inflation, or tariffs added to a potential demand slowdown could mean a cut in tech spending. Early signs of a flattening demand in the restaurant sector overall are visible from a Gross Payment Volume increase of only 23%, where net location additions were 24% - implying a potential flattening/slowing Gross Payment Volume per location.

The other thing I am cautious about is Toast's high beta of ~3.5 in the past year or so. That is, of course, a function of the rally it saw in the period. But any broad market correction or consolidation may drag Toast far more, if there are any execution missteps or a macro that's not conducive. In the short term I see demand pressures as real, while costs will be difficult to optimize further, given Toast's growth stage. That could dampen margins. Any backward step will open up the power of the high beta on the downside as well.

There are a few positive macro and secular tailwind factors too. In the immediate term, rate cuts could be more supportive (if not fueling further valuation expansion). That could balance out any macro weakness. The long term secular tailwinds from digitization and AI/automation is positive too. But that goes through near-term margin pressure as Toast accesses newer verticals and products.

Valuations Still High

Despite some contraction post-Q2 earnings results and a consolidation from the 2024 year end highs, the EV to forward revenues remain elevated at a close to 4x ratio. Since Toast sits in the middle of pure SaaS-like plays that could demand higher multiples and purer payment processors like Shift4 and Block, it is difficult to gauge whether the valuations are in bubble territory from peer comparisons. However, for perspective, Block and Shift4 are both trading at ~1.9x in EV to forward revenue terms. On the other hand, a pure SaaS play like Shopify trades at ~15-16x multiples.

This shows there is scope for Toast for valuation re-ratings, even if minor SaaS like characteristics start showing up. As of now, that means more subscription led growth and better margins. If Toast continues to show better margins, valuations will not only be sustainable, but have decent room to grow.

The immediate future is not likely to be best placed to test those executional improvements. If margins need to improve towards SaaS-like numbers (even marginally towards may do), the topline has to keep growing at the same pace as today. The macro environment is not supportive there.

Competition is also stiff, and remains a long-term pressure on Toast. With many existing strong players like Block, Square, and even Oracle in the same market, Toast will have to defend its moat through continuous innovation and customer stickiness.

A Hold - Buy on Dips

Overall, I think Toast is best rated a Hold at current valuations. There is long-term potential, but the immediate term may not be supportive. Toast is also in a low margin and demand sensitive cyclical end customer segment that limits its runaway margin or topline expansion potential at a scale that current valuations expect. Particularly given competition will always keep pricing pressures intact and product relevance will require continual investing.

Having said so, there is no scope for faulting Toast's operational excellence so far. There is only a need to enter at the right valuations. I believe, aggressive investors can enter even today after the minor pullback from recent highs, but I will wait for EV to forward revenues of around at least 3.5x or below for a first buy on dip position. Historically, even those levels are not rock bottom for Toast. Buy on dip and accumulate further on further dips is easier said than done, particularly for a high beta stock that could really correct significantly if momentum slows, but given Toast's execution so far and a secular digitization tailwind, I remain relatively comfortable for long-term positions entered at the right valuations (or at least more reasonable).
